item.title: Turla StockStay, Windchill RCE, Linux pedit COW/DirtyClone, and Miasma Supply Chain Attacks
item.content:encoded:
CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue — Must Read

CISA added the critical PTC Windchill and FlexPLM RCE flaw CVE-2026-12569 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after attackers began actively using it to deploy JSP web shells. Organizations should patch immediately and check for the listed indicators of compromise, especially suspicious JSP files under the Windchill login path.

  • CISA added CVE-2026-12569, a critical remote code execution vulnerability affecting PTC Windchill PDMlink and PTC FlexPLM, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog due to evidence of active exploitation.
  • PTC described CVE-2026-12569 as an improper input validation issue that may be exploited through deserialization of untrusted data to execute arbitrary code.
  • PTC said that, despite patches being released last week, it continued to receive reports of heightened threat activity as of June 25.
  • Unknown attackers are exploiting the vulnerability to deploy JSP web shells against susceptible systems.
  • PTC published indicators of compromise and mitigation steps, including blocking 5.180.41.35, checking for suspicious JSP files under /Windchill/login/, and adding a rule to block requests containing the X-windchill-req header.

New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries — Must Read

A Linux kernel flaw dubbed pedit COW can let local unprivileged users gain root by poisoning cached setuid binaries in memory, with public exploit code already available. Patch and reboot affected systems, or mitigate by disabling act_pedit or unprivileged user namespaces where feasible.

  • CVE-2026-46331, nicknamed pedit COW, is an out-of-bounds write in the Linux kernel act_pedit component that can let a local unprivileged user gain root on affected systems.
  • A public working exploit appeared within a day of the CVE assignment on June 16 and poisons the cached in-memory copy of the setuid root binary /bin/su without modifying the file on disk.
  • The exploit requires act_pedit to be loadable and unprivileged user namespaces to be available, giving the attacker namespace-local CAP_NET_ADMIN needed to trigger the bug.
  • The PoC author reported successful unprivileged-to-root exploitation on RHEL 10 and Debian 13, while Ubuntu 24.04 required routing execution through permissive AppArmor profiles and Ubuntu 26.04 blocks that path by default.
  • The article recommends installing a patched kernel and rebooting, or mitigating by blocking act_pedit from loading or disabling unprivileged user namespaces if patching is not yet possible.

Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack — Must Read

The Miasma/Mini Shai-Hulud supply chain campaign has compromised new npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and a Go-related repository to steal developer credentials and propagate through trusted software ecosystems. The key risk is that the malware targets developer workflows broadly, using install-time execution, CI/CD secret theft, and GitHub dead drops to spread beyond a single package registry.

  • Cybersecurity researchers reported a new wave of activity linked to the Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware family affecting npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and a Go module associated with the Verana Blockchain project.
  • Socket said the campaign’s goal is to harvest developer or maintainer credentials and use the stolen data to spread across package registries, repositories, and trusted developer workflows.
  • The affected npm packages include LeoPlatform and RStreams packages, and researchers suspect an npm developer account associated with LeoPlatform maintainer czirker was breached to push trojanized versions.
  • The malicious npm packages use a binding.gyp file to execute code during installation, launch a JavaScript loader, install the Bun runtime if needed, and run a stealer payload for secrets, credentials, and tokens.
  • The malware also drops a GitHub Actions workflow named “Run Copilot” to capture CI/CD environment secrets and uploads stolen information to public GitHub repositories with the description “Alright Lets See If This Works.”

New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets — Should Read

DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503) is a Linux kernel flaw that can let local users gain root by corrupting file-backed memory through cloned network packets, especially on systems allowing unprivileged user namespaces. Administrators should install patched kernels or temporarily restrict user namespaces and affected networking modules.

  • DirtyClone, tracked as CVE-2026-43503 with a CVSS score of 8.8, lets a local user corrupt file-backed memory through a cloned network packet and gain root on affected Linux systems.
  • Exploitation requires CAP_NET_ADMIN to configure a loopback IPsec tunnel, and unprivileged user namespaces enabled by default on Debian and Fedora can allow a local user to obtain that capability inside a new namespace.
  • Ubuntu 24.04 and later restrict namespace creation via AppArmor, blocking the default exploit path described in the article.
  • The affected systems include multi-tenant servers, CI runners, container hosts, and Kubernetes clusters where untrusted users can create namespaces.
  • The fix landed upstream in Linux v7.1-rc5 and has been backported to stable and LTS branches, with advisories published by Ubuntu, Debian, and SUSE and a Red Hat Bugzilla tracking entry.

Turla group adds more malware to Russia’s espionage efforts against Ukraine — Should Read

Google researchers say Russia-linked Turla has been developing and using a malware strain called StockStay since at least 2022 to spy mainly on Ukrainian government and defense targets. The tool appears designed as a parallel replacement or backup to Turla’s older Kazuar malware, helping the group maintain persistent access even when other tools are detected.

  • Google researchers said the Russian state-backed Turla group has been developing and deploying a malware strain called StockStay since at least December 2022.
  • StockStay was primarily used to target Ukrainian government and defense organizations, with early samples also identified in Italy, the Netherlands, Poland, and Germany.
  • Google said StockStay shares significant code and functionality with Kazuar, another Turla malware framework previously used against military and defense targets in Ukraine.
  • Victims were typically infected through phishing emails containing malicious Remote Desktop Protocol configuration files that connected compromised computers to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
  • Researchers said Turla used academic and diplomatic themes in phishing campaigns, including emails from a compromised Ukrainian university account and abuse of a diplomatic education platform.

Russia used social engineering to breach prominent messaging accounts, Ukraine says — Should Read

Ukraine’s security agency says Russia ran a long-term social engineering campaign, uncovered with the FBI, to hijack messaging accounts of officials, military personnel, politicians and activists in Ukraine, Europe and the U.S. The attackers impersonated platform support staff to steal credentials or verification codes rather than exploiting flaws in apps like Signal, WhatsApp or Telegram.

  • Ukraine's Security Service said it uncovered, together with the FBI, a long-running Russian campaign to compromise messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians and activists in Ukraine, Europe and the United States.
  • The SBU said the campaign aimed to access sensitive military, political and economic information exchanged through messaging applications and to steal victims' personal data.
  • The attackers used social engineering techniques, including text messages impersonating official messaging platform support services and urging users to disclose account credentials.
  • The SBU said the messages were sent in the morning hours, when users were particularly vulnerable due to their physical and emotional state.
  • The SBU did not identify the Russian intelligence service responsible, specify which messaging platforms were primarily targeted or say how many victims had been affected.

FCC votes to toughen rules in bid to better protect undersea cables — Should Read

The FCC voted to tighten oversight of undersea internet cables by banning equipment from China and other foreign adversaries, adding licensing for key cable terminal equipment, and speeding approvals for trusted U.S.-based operators. The move aims to protect critical global internet infrastructure from spying, cyber disruption, and physical sabotage while supporting faster cable expansion.

  • The FCC voted to strengthen oversight of undersea submarine cables that carry nearly all internet traffic.
  • The new rules will block Chinese firms and companies from other countries deemed foreign adversaries from selling equipment related to undersea cables.
  • The FCC plans to require owners and operators of submarine line terminal equipment, which connects undersea cables to U.S. terrestrial facilities, to be licensed.
  • Cable operators that certify high security standards, have operated cables without incident, and accept ongoing oversight may avoid intensive approval reviews.
  • FCC Chair Brendan Carr said submarine cables face growing risks from cyber and physical disruptions and from bad actors seeking access to sensitive data and communications.

Russia accuses Apple of ‘political censorship’ after VK apps removed from App Store — May Read

Russia accused Apple of political censorship after the company removed major VK apps from the App Store, with Apple reportedly saying the removals were required to comply with sanctions. The move affects downloads and updates for VK services on Apple devices, though already installed apps continue to work.

  • Apple removed VKontakte, VK Music, VK Messenger, VK Video, Odnoklassniki, and Mail.ru services from its App Store, making them unavailable for download or updates on Apple devices.
  • Apple told BBC News Russia that it removed the applications to comply with sanctions regulations and follows the laws of the jurisdictions in which it operates, but did not specify which sanctions applied.
  • VK said Apple removed the apps without prior notice or explanation and called the decision unmotivated and unacceptable, arguing that the company itself had never been placed under sanctions.
  • Russian officials criticized the removal, with Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova calling it an act of political censorship and Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov questioning Apple's reliability.
  • VK said already installed apps would continue to function, while Android users could still download the services through RuStore, Google Play, Huawei AppGallery, Samsung Store, Xiaomi Store, and VK's official websites.

A Construction Bid Invitation Hid a Compromised Website Behind a Legitimate-Looking PDF Label — May Read

A phishing email disguised as a construction bid invitation used legitimate-looking PDF link text while hiding a URL to a compromised Brazilian website. Standard email authentication passed, so the key detection signal was the mismatch between the displayed document label and the actual link destination.

  • A phishing email formatted as a construction bid invitation was sent via blind carbon copy, with one named recipient used as the envelope address.
  • The visible link text was formatted as a bid-preview PDF filename, but the actual URL pointed to a path on a compromised Brazilian website unrelated to construction, the sender, or the recipient’s industry.
  • SPF, DKIM, and ARC all passed, the sender domain had no flagged reputation, and Microsoft’s spam confidence level was set to 1.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message as a suspicious link based on the mismatch between the link’s display text and its actual destination.
  • The article states that link-text consistency is an independent detection signal from link reputation because a clean-reputation domain can still be used in a phishing link.

Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance — Optional

AI agents are rapidly spreading through enterprises while inheriting human and service-account permissions that traditional IAM tools cannot monitor once actions begin. The article argues organizations need “guardian agents” to continuously discover, baseline, and enforce least privilege for autonomous AI activity at runtime.

  • AI agents in enterprise environments can inherit permissions from human or service identities and operate across multiple systems without the governance checkpoints used for traditional human access.
  • Traditional IAM, IGA, PAM, and CIEM tools are described as insufficient for agentic workloads because they focus on provisioning, authentication, vaulting, or platform-specific entitlements rather than runtime behavior across applications.
  • The article defines guardian agents as a control layer that continuously discovers AI agents, maps them to owners and inherited permissions, builds behavioral baselines, detects anomalies, and enforces least privilege during execution.
  • Unmanaged AI agents can create risks including over-privileged identities, orphaned sessions, stale credentials, prompt injection, and lateral movement through chained agent calls.
  • The article says organizations should bring AI agents under governance by discovering what is running, classifying agents by trust level and permission scope, enforcing least privilege at runtime, and integrating agent identity data with existing IAM and IGA systems.

Healthcare Cybersecurity in an Era of Geopolitical Instability — Optional

Healthcare organizations, especially in Europe, can no longer assume they are outside the scope of geopolitical cyber conflict. They need to factor geopolitical risk into governance, supply chains, third-party dependencies, and resilience planning.

  • Healthcare has traditionally been viewed as a protected, civilian, neutral, and essential sector, but that assumption is increasingly being tested.
  • The current geopolitical environment is marked by sustained instability, multiple military conflicts, and rising tensions across regions.
  • Cyber operations have become a persistent instrument of statecraft, and these developments are interconnected with geopolitical instability.
  • The article says healthcare is no longer outside the scope of geopolitical cyber operations.
  • European healthcare organisations must consider how geopolitical risk influences their threat exposure, supply chains, and dependencies on external providers.

item.title: Turla STOCKSTAY Targets Ukraine as Cellebrite Russia Use and Packagist Malware Blocking Emerge
Poland arrests SIM-swapping gang; Anthropic tests mobile Claude Cowork
Shop Callback Phishing, SaaS Cookie Theft, and Windows 10 ESU Updates
Gaslight macOS Malware, Bluekit BITM Phishing, and Chrome Ad Blocker Injection Risks
DCloud Scam Infrastructure, curl CVE-2026-8932, Hoppscotch CVE-2026-50160, and Ukrposhta Cyberattack
Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke; Leo Platform npm Packages Compromised
